annotations.label: Slope as a function of the x-coordinate

anchor.snippet: of a graph at a point was regarded as a function of the x -coordinate of the point

annotations.note: The slope of the tangent as a function of x is formalized as the derivative function `deriv`, though the historical geometric framing is informal.

annotations.label: Modern concept of a function

anchor.snippet: a single-valued mapping from one set to another

annotations.note: A single-valued mapping is Lean's primitive function type `α → β`, a foundational notion rather than a defined Mathlib declaration, though extensive function infrastructure exists.

annotations.label: Sharaf al-Din's analysis of solutions

anchor.snippet: A value less than d means no positive solution; a value equal to d corresponds to one solution

annotations.note: This specific historical analysis of the number of positive roots of a cubic via its maximum is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bernoulli's definition of a function (1718)

anchor.snippet: any expression made up of a variable and some constants

annotations.note: Bernoulli's prose definition of a function as an expression has no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Differentiable functions

anchor.snippet: The functions considered in those times are called today differentiable functions

annotations.note: Differentiable functions are formalized as `Differentiable`, generalized to functions between normed spaces.

annotations.label: Euler's definition of a function (1748)

anchor.snippet: an analytic expression composed in any way whatsoever

annotations.note: Euler's historical notion of an 'analytic expression' is a prose definition, distinct from Mathlib's modern analytic-function concept.

annotations.label: Euler's multi-valued functions

anchor.snippet: multi-valued functions whose values are determined by an implicit equation

annotations.note: The implicit function theorem yields single-valued local solutions of implicit equations (`ImplicitFunctionData`), but genuinely multi-valued functions are not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Euler's general definition (1755)

anchor.snippet: then the first are called functions of the second

annotations.note: Euler's 1755 prose definition of dependent variables has no specific Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Fourier's representation claim

anchor.snippet: an arbitrary function could be represented by a Fourier series

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Fourier series converge for L² functions (`hasSum_fourier_series_L2`), the corrected form of Fourier's overly broad claim about arbitrary functions.

annotations.label: Cauchy's notion of a function

anchor.snippet: he normally regards a function as being defined by an analytic expression

annotations.note: Cauchy's historical conception of a function as an analytic expression is not formalized.

annotations.label: Modern formal definition of a function

anchor.snippet: a relation in which every first element has a unique second element

annotations.note: Relations are formalized as `Rel := α → β → Prop`, but the single-valued-relation definition of 'function' is not Mathlib's primitive function notion.

annotations.label: Lobachevsky's definition (1834)

anchor.snippet: a function of x be defined as a number given for each x and varying gradually with x

annotations.note: Lobachevsky's 1834 prose definition has no specific Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Dirichlet's continuous function (1837)

anchor.snippet: y is called a continuous function of x for this interval

annotations.note: Continuity is formalized as `Continuous`, generalizing Dirichlet's interval-based ε-style notion to arbitrary topological spaces.

annotations.label: Dedekind's definition of a function

anchor.snippet: a function as a single-valued mapping between any two sets

annotations.note: Dedekind's single-valued mapping coincides with Lean's primitive function type `α → β` rather than a named Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Hardy's definition of a function

anchor.snippet: a function as a relation between two variables x and y

annotations.note: Hardy's prose definition of a function as a relation between variables has no specific Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: De Morgan's propositional form

anchor.snippet: into a form such as "X is Y", where the symbols

annotations.note: De Morgan's syntactic 'X is Y' propositional form is a historical logical notation, not formalized.

annotations.label: Boole's class symbolism for oxen and horses

anchor.snippet: the class oxen by x and that of horses by y

annotations.note: This illustrative example of Boole's class notation is not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Boole's definition of a function (c.1849)

anchor.snippet: is said to be a function of it

annotations.note: Boole's historical prose definition of a function has no specific Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Boole's algebraic definition of f(x)

anchor.section: George Boole's The Laws of Thought 1854; John Venn's Symbolic Logic 1881
anchor.snippet: Any algebraic expression involving symbol x is termed a function of x

annotations.note: Boole's definition of a function as 'any algebraic expression in x' is a historical prose definition without a Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Boole's logical operations as algebra

anchor.section: George Boole's The Laws of Thought 1854; John Venn's Symbolic Logic 1881
anchor.snippet: is logical NOT( x ), xy is the logical AND

annotations.note: Boole's algebraic rendering of logical operations (xy = AND, 1−x = NOT) is captured abstractly by `BooleanRing` (and `BooleanAlgebra`).

annotations.label: Venn's logical function as a class

anchor.section: George Boole's The Laws of Thought 1854; John Venn's Symbolic Logic 1881
anchor.snippet: he equated unequivocally the notion of

annotations.note: Venn's identification of a logical function with a class is a historical conceptual claim, not formalized.

annotations.label: Frege's argument and function

anchor.snippet: replacing them with argument and function respectively

annotations.note: Frege's argument/function analysis of judgments is a historical logical doctrine without a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Frege's lighter-than carbon dioxide function

anchor.snippet: stable component, representing the totality of

annotations.note: This illustrative Fregean example is not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Frege's one- and two-argument function symbolism

anchor.snippet: where A is the argument and

annotations.note: Frege's notation for one- and two-argument functions is a historical symbolism, not formalized.

annotations.label: Peano's function presign

anchor.snippet: Given all these conditions are met

annotations.note: Peano's 'presign' function notation is a historical symbolism without a Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Russell's propositional functions

anchor.section: Bertrand Russell's The Principles of Mathematics 1903
anchor.snippet: these generalizations Russell named propositional functions

annotations.note: A propositional function corresponds to a predicate `α → Prop`, a foundational type-theoretic notion rather than a named Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Frege's 2x³+x function essence

anchor.section: Bertrand Russell's The Principles of Mathematics 1903
anchor.snippet: is what is left when the x is taken away

annotations.note: The philosophical claim about the 'essence' of a function after removing its argument is not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Propositional functions as fundamental

anchor.snippet: the fundamental kind from which the more usual kinds of function

annotations.note: Russell's philosophical claim that propositional functions are the fundamental kind of function is not formalized.

annotations.label: Russell's "ŷ is hurt" propositional function

anchor.snippet: Substitution of one of these values for variable ŷ yields a proposition

annotations.note: This illustrative example of substitution into a propositional function is not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Russell's descriptive function

anchor.snippet: defines a descriptive function (pages 30ff) as the unique value

annotations.note: The 'unique value satisfying a relation' relates to `ExistsUnique` and choice operators, but Russell's definite-description (iota) operator is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Hilbert's logical ε-function

anchor.section: Formalist's function: David Hilbert's axiomatization of mathematics (1904–1927)
anchor.snippet: let us call ε the logical ε-function

annotations.note: Hilbert's ε-operator is Lean's core `Classical.epsilon` (built on indefinite description), used throughout Mathlib (e.g. Mathlib.ModelTheory.Skolem).

annotations.label: Effectively calculable function

anchor.section: Formalist's function: David Hilbert's axiomatization of mathematics (1904–1927)
anchor.snippet: define what was meant by an

annotations.note: The class of effectively calculable functions is formalized as the partial recursive / computable functions (`Partrec`, `Computable`).

annotations.label: Church's thesis

anchor.section: Formalist's function: David Hilbert's axiomatization of mathematics (1904–1927)
anchor.snippet: this class of functions exhausts all the number-theoretic functions

annotations.note: The Church–Turing thesis is an informal claim identifying intuitive computability with recursiveness and cannot be stated as a formal theorem.

annotations.label: Turing's unsolvability result

anchor.section: Formalist's function: David Hilbert's axiomatization of mathematics (1904–1927)
anchor.snippet: there can be no general process for determining whether a given formula

annotations.note: The unsolvability of the Entscheidungsproblem is captured by the formalized non-computability of the halting problem (`halting_problem`), together with Rice's theorem.

annotations.label: Russell's paradox

anchor.snippet: to be a predicate that cannot be predicated of itself

annotations.note: Russell's paradox is not formalized (Lean's type-theoretic foundations preclude unrestricted comprehension), though the related Burali-Forti paradox and Cantor's theorem are.

annotations.label: Zermelo's Axiom of Separation

anchor.section: Zermelo's set theory (1908) modified by Skolem (1922)
anchor.snippet: M possesses a subset M Φ containing as elements precisely those elements

annotations.note: The separation/specification schema is realized by `ZFSet.sep` together with the characterization `ZFSet.mem_sep`.

annotations.label: Skolem's definite proposition

anchor.section: Zermelo's set theory (1908) modified by Skolem (1922)
anchor.snippet: a finite expression constructed from elementary propositions of the form

annotations.note: Skolem's replacement of 'definite property' by first-order definability corresponds to Mathlib's first-order `BoundedFormula`/`Formula`, though 'definite proposition' itself is not a named decl.

annotations.label: Russell's ordered couple with sense

anchor.snippet: it may also be called an ordered couple

annotations.note: An ordered couple 'with sense' is the ordered pair, realized as the core type `Prod`; Russell's specific set-theoretic construction is not used.

annotations.label: Hausdorff's ordered pair

anchor.snippet: gave the definition of the ordered pair

annotations.note: Mathlib's set-theoretic ordered pair uses Kuratowski's construction, not Hausdorff's specific definition with distinguished elements.

annotations.label: Kuratowski's ordered pair

anchor.snippet: Kuratowski (1921) offered a definition that has been widely used ever since

annotations.note: `ZFSet.pair x y = {{x}, {x, y}}` is exactly Kuratowski's definition of the ordered pair.

annotations.label: Schönfinkel's many-one correspondence

anchor.section: Schönfinkel's notion of function as a many-one correspondence 1924
anchor.snippet: by function we mean in the simplest case a correspondence between the elements of some domain

annotations.note: A function as a many-one correspondence is Lean's primitive `α → β`; Schönfinkel's associated currying idea is realized as `Function.curry`.

annotations.label: Von Neumann's function as set of pairs

anchor.snippet: since a function can be regarded as a set of pairs

annotations.note: A function's graph as a set of pairs is `PFun.graph`/`Set.graphOn`, but Mathlib's functions are primitive rather than literally sets of pairs.

annotations.label: Bourbaki's functional relation (1939)

anchor.snippet: is called a functional relation in y if, for all

annotations.note: Single-valued (functional) relations correspond to `Rel`/`PFun`, but they are not Mathlib's defining notion of a function.

annotations.label: Set-theoretic function as a relation

anchor.snippet: define a relation as a set of ordered pairs

annotations.note: A relation as a set of ordered pairs is `Rel := α → β → Prop`; defining functions via such relations is not Mathlib's primitive approach.

annotations.label: Kleene's predicate as propositional function

anchor.snippet: The predicate is a propositional function of one variable

annotations.note: A predicate (propositional function of one variable) is the type `α → Prop` in Lean, a foundational notion rather than a named declaration.

annotations.label: Bourbaki's function as a triple (1954)

anchor.snippet: gave a definition of a function as a triple

annotations.note: Mathlib functions are primitive `α → β` carrying their domain and codomain as types, not modeled as explicit (domain, codomain, graph) triples.

annotations.label: Suppes's function definition (1960)

anchor.snippet: a function (p. 86) as a relation where no two pairs have the same first member

annotations.note: Single-valued relations (no two pairs sharing a first member) correspond to `PFun`/`Rel`, but functions remain primitive in Lean.

annotations.label: Tarski's sentential function

anchor.snippet: which contains variables and, on replacement of these variables by constants becomes a sentence

annotations.note: Open formulas with free variables that become sentences upon substitution are formalized as `BoundedFormula`/`Formula` (closed ones being `Sentence`).

annotations.label: Tarski's functional relation

anchor.snippet: Tarski calls the relational form of function a

annotations.note: The relational form of a function corresponds to `Rel`, but it is not Mathlib's primitive function notion.
